content: Never commit more than 5% of your total capital to a single trade idea. If you take positions in two related grain markets, that is still ONE idea. This rule survived Marcus blowing up twice by betting everything on one trade.

Always use protective stops. Actually place them in the market — a mental stop is not enough for most traders. The stop commits you to exit at a predetermined point. Never enter a position without knowing your exit point first.

If a position doesn't feel right as soon as you put it on, get out immediately. Don't be embarrassed to change your mind within minutes. When in doubt, get out and get a good night's sleep. You can always come back in. While in, you can't think clearly.

Holding winners is equally important as cutting losers. If you don't stay with your winners, you cannot pay for the losers. Marcus's most devastating experience was a PROFITABLE trade where he got out prematurely — soybeans went limit-up 12 more days.

Follow your own light. Even following talented traders' ideas leads to problems — you combine worst elements of both traders. If it's not your own idea, you lack courage to hold through adversity. Trade your own analysis.

Best trades have ALL THREE elements: (1) fundamentals suggest supply/demand imbalance, (2) chart confirms direction, (3) market tone is correct — bull market shrugs off bearish news, responds vigorously to bullish news. Restrict activity to only these setups.

On the 3rd consecutive limit-up day, become very cautious. Almost always get out on the 4th limit-up day. Mandatory rule: force yourself out on the 5th consecutive limit-up day. This volatility signal indicates blowoff tops.

When news is wonderful and a market can't go up, be sure to be short. When a market acts terribly relative to everything else on a powerful day, put down a bet against it. Divergence between correlated markets signals major turns.

Track your equity curve. If the trend in your equity is down, that is a sign to cut back and reevaluate. If losing money faster than you made it, that is a warning signal. Marcus used equity plotting extensively.

Losing begets losing. When you start losing, it triggers negative psychology and pessimism. During losing streaks: cut back rapidly, stop completely if it gets bad enough. Take 3-4 weeks off to reset. Don't fight back by trading heavier.

All successful traders who lasted got balanced lives with fun outside trading. You can't sustain trading if you don't have other focus. Eventually you wind up overtrading or getting excessively disturbed about temporary failures.

Kovner risks no more than 1% of portfolio on any single trade. He studies correlation of trades daily to reduce exposure. Eight highly correlated positions = one position eight times as large. A mistake in position correlation is root of serious problems.

Place stops beyond technical barriers, not within trading ranges. Allocate max dollar risk to fewer contracts with wider stops — the REVERSE of typical trader who limits loss per contract but trades maximum contracts. Stop should indicate trade is wrong, not just max loss per contract.

Undertrade, undertrade, undertrade. Whatever you think your position should be, cut it at least in half. Novice traders trade 3-5x too big, taking 5-10% risks when they should take 1-2% risks. Position size is determined by the stop distance.

Don't personalize the market. The market is totally impersonal; it doesn't care whether you make money. Whenever a trader says 'I wish' or 'I hope,' they engage in destructive thinking that takes attention away from diagnosis.

When something disturbs your emotional equilibrium and sense of what the world is like, close ALL positions related to that event. Kovner's first rule: don't get caught in situations where you can lose a great deal for reasons you don't understand.

Technical analysis is like a thermometer — it tracks the past, does not predict the future. Fundamentalists ignoring charts are like doctors refusing to take a patient's temperature. Use charts to observe unusual behavior and disequilibria.

Breakouts from tight consolidations where nobody understands the reason are usually good risk/reward trades. The LESS explanation for a price move, the MORE significant it likely is. If everyone observes a pattern, odds of false signal increase (Heisenberg principle).

Imagine alternative scenarios. Try them on one at a time. Most will be wrong. But when one picture has 9 out of 10 elements clicking, that becomes your world reality. Keep testing scenarios until one is confirmed by the market.

Track guru/newsletter consensus. When most advisors are bullish but market is not moving up, and you have fundamental reason to be bearish, feel much stronger about the trade. Look for consensus the market is NOT confirming.

The market usually leads because there are people who know more than you. Governments occasionally have advance information. Large price moves against you that you don't understand — get out and look for reason later. Big players tip their hand in price.

Pay strict attention to correlations of all positions. Measure total portfolio risk daily. In bear markets, use sharp countertrend rallies to enter positions — bear markets have sharp down moves followed by quick retracements unlike bull markets.

Create scenarios for each market at least weekly. Define expected ranges and what to do if price breaks out of those ranges. Have predetermined action plans — don't make decisions in the heat of the moment.

Dennis: Go with the trend. Whatever method you use, if there is a major trend, your approach should assure you get in. Being consistent and doing it all the time is more important than the particular characteristics you use to define the trend.

A strong Friday close at yearly highs is significant. At minimum, do NOT hold a short position with a loss if market closes at a Friday high, or a long if it closes at a Friday low. Weekend gap risk favors the trend direction.

After a destabilizing loss, get out, go home, take a nap. Put time between the loss and your next decision. Don't try to catch up or double up to recoup losses. A certain amount of loss affects judgment — you need recovery time.

Preserve capital for the few instances where you can make a lot in a very short time. What you can't afford is throwing away capital on suboptimal trades — if you do, you'll be too debilitated when the right position comes along.

Expect the extreme. Don't think in terms of boundaries that limit what the market might do. The unexpected and impossible happen regularly. Don't draw boundaries from history — soybeans at $4 'couldn't go higher' then went to $12.97.

You could publish trading rules in the newspaper and no one would follow them. The key is consistency and discipline. Almost anyone can make rules that are 80% as good as what Dennis taught. What they can't do is give confidence to stick to rules when things go bad.

95% of profits come from only 5% of trades. Missing only a few major profit opportunities has dramatic negative impact. Guard against holding too rigid opinions that could cause you to miss a major trend. Don't miss the big ones.

When losing, you should be thinking about trading the MOST. Times when you least want to think about it are precisely when you need to focus most. Don't stick your head in the sand and hope it gets better.
If you have a loss after a week or two, you are clearly wrong. Even when around breakeven but significant time has passed, you are probably wrong too. Time-based stops complement price-based stops.

Don't be a hero. Don't have an ego. Always question yourself and your ability. Don't ever feel that you are very good. The second you do, you are dead. PTJ's biggest hits always came after great periods when he started thinking he knew something.

Don't ever average losers. Decrease trading volume when trading poorly; increase when trading well. Never trade in situations where you don't have control — don't risk significant money in front of key reports, that is gambling not trading.

Always think of your entry point as last night's close. Don't be concerned about where you got into a position. The only relevant question is whether you are bullish or bearish on the position TODAY. This prevents anchoring bias.

The most important rule of trading is to play great defense, not great offense. Every day assume every position you have is wrong. Know where your stop risk points are. Define maximum possible drawdown. Spend rest of day enjoying positions going your way.

PTJ limits monthly losses: 3% stop on equity for the rest of the month if down. Never have a double-digit monthly loss. Reduce position size when trading poorly — trade SMALLEST position when trading is WORST.

After devastating loss, play very small — shrink to 1/5 or 1/10 normal position. Get black ink (any profit) to rebuild rhythm and confidence. It's not how much you make, but getting your confidence back. Works consistently.

Biggest losses have ALWAYS followed largest profits. After strong profit run, play smaller rather than larger. Take a day off as reward. Battle fatigue sets in after ~12 profitable days in a row. Complacency from winning leads to sloppy trading.

When nervous about overnight/weekend position and market lets you out at much better price than expected, you are usually better off STAYING with the position. When your worst fears aren't realized, consider INCREASING position.

Always liquidate half position below new highs/lows, remaining half beyond that point. Floor traders buy to trigger stops at obvious levels then liquidate into them. Don't be the one getting stopped at the exact high/low.

Markets look their very best when setting new highs — that is often the BEST time to sell. To be a good trader you must be a contrarian. The best money is made at market turns, not by catching trends in the middle.

Hite controls risk with four principles: (1) Never trade counter to trend, (2) Max 1% risk per trade, (3) Extreme diversification across ~60 markets and multiple systems, (4) Track volatility to liquidate when risk/reward exceeds limits.

Look for hardiest method, not optimum method. Anyone can devise perfect system for the past. We don't trade markets, we trade money. Gold and cocoa are both 1% bets — they are the same. Risk is risk regardless of the instrument.

When market doesn't respond to important news the way it SHOULD, it tells you something very important. Iran/Iraq war broke out, gold only up $1 — great sale. When a market makes a historic high, something HAS changed regardless of what experts say.

You can risk 1% of capital, 5%, or 10% — but realize the more you risk, the more volatile the results. The relationship between risk percentage and drawdown volatility is nonlinear. Seykota's key insight on position sizing.
If you don't bet, you can't win. If you lose all your chips, you can't bet. These two rules encompass all of risk management and opportunity management. Never forget both sides of this equation.

Because they would rather lose money than admit they're wrong. 'I'll get out when I'm even' protects the ego. Became a winning trader when able to say: to hell with my ego, making money is more important. Schwartz's key transformation.

Always check charts and moving averages before taking position. Is price above or below the moving average? That works better than any tool. Don't go against the moving averages — it is self-destructive. Schwartz's primary indicator.

Don't increase position size until you have doubled or tripled your capital. Most people increase bets as soon as they start making money — that is a quick way to get wiped out. Build the cushion first.

Bottom fishing is one of the most expensive forms of gambling. Only acceptable with sufficient justification and a predetermined plan with specific price levels written in advance. Don't do it impulsively.

When T-bonds and T-bills differ in their relationship to moving average — one above, one below — have NO position until one confirms the other. Interest rates can't move far until both confirm. Divergence = danger.

Do nothing unless there is something to do. Most people always have to be playing. They make a big play then rush out to do something else. Wait until there is money lying in the corner, then go pick it up. Trade as little as possible. Rogers.

Go against hysteria and panic — 95% of the time you will make money if you can stick it out. When market moves in gaps, it's hysteria. But first examine the situation fully. Just because you see panic doesn't automatically mean you should trade against it.

Whenever buying, make sure you're not going to lose money first. If there is very good value, you probably won't lose much even if wrong. Always need a catalyst — bottoming markets can go nowhere for years without one.

The market will go higher than you think it can, and lower than you think it will. Most people don't have the foresight to look 6 months or 2 years out. Just because you know something doesn't mean everyone does. Rogers' Memorex lesson.

Always invest against central banks. When central banks try to prop up a currency, go the other way. Government measures to counteract a trend = sell the rally after the government action. The trend will prevail. Rogers' axiom.

Be flexible and alert to investing in anything. No boundaries. Many people say 'I could never buy that' or 'I could never go short.' Biases against certain markets or trade types limit your field of opportunity. Rogers trades everything globally.

There is no such thing as a paper loss. A paper loss is a very real loss. Don't comfort yourself with 'it's just a paper loss.' If fundamentally correct, sit there and let market hysteria wash around you. If wrong, first loss is the best loss.

If you make 50% two years in a row then lose 50% the third year, you are worse off than a money market fund. Wait for something you know is right. Take profit, put it back in money market, wait again. You come out way ahead.

Weinstein's devastating soybean loss: had a specific dollar target ($350K for a castle) that was driving position size, not market analysis. Never set specific dollar targets for trades. Let market conditions determine when to exit, not your spending desires.

Trading is very competitive and you have to handle getting your butt kicked. There are enormous emotional ups and downs. The emotional burden is substantial — if you personalize losses, you can't trade. Tullis taught PTJ steel-hard emotional control.

When trading size, get out when the MARKET lets you out, not when you want to get out. To move a large position, don't wait until market is at new high/low where little volume trades. Liquidate half before the stop cluster zone, half beyond it.

The vast majority of all long option positions lose money (90% per SEC study). Therefore 90% of all SHORT option positions make money. If you want to be bearish, sell calls rather than buying puts. Rogers' option philosophy.

Being a trader is like being a boxer — every now and then the market gives you a good wallop. After twenty years you get a bit punch-drunk. The shock absorbers deteriorate over time. Dennis' honest assessment of long-term trading toll.

Avoid the intermediate term — it picks up the vast majority of trend followers. Be as short-term or as long-term as you can stand. The best strategy is to avoid the middle like the plague. Dennis' key insight on timeframe selection.

Portfolio insurance was a terrible idea — insurance in name only, just a massive stop-loss order. Portfolio insurers came in with billions of dollars of sales in a few hours, market unable to absorb. Systematic selling into declining markets amplifies crashes.

If everybody believes there is no reason for a market to break out and it suddenly does, the chances of an important underlying cause are much greater. Observed breakouts driven by newspaper stories are less reliable than unexplained ones.

Steinhardt reviews portfolio SIX times daily. He is a negative monitor — only talks to people when things are lousy or stocks aren't acting as they should. Constant scrutiny prevents small problems from becoming large ones.

Variant perception: develop perceptions at variance with the general market view. Play those variant perceptions until they are no longer variant. The key is the perception must be CONTRARIAN — if everyone agrees with you, the edge is gone.

When stocks don't act as fundamentals suggest, investigate what the other side knows. Assume the person on other side of your trade knows at least as much as you do. If stock goes against you, find out what their perception was.

Being contrarian does NOT automatically mean victory. Many contrarians bought bonds at 8%, 9%, 10% yields and lost. You need RIGHT TIMING and APPROPRIATE SIZE. Process requires courage, commitment, and understanding of your own psychology.

If you need to hedge a position so badly that you grasp at a second stock as hedge, you're giving yourself two problems instead of one. If your problem is that great, address it directly. Don't compound a mistake. Steinhardt on hedging.

Don't trade when you can't afford to lose. Trading with scared money guarantees failure: you miss best opportunities (most risky), jump out of good positions prematurely, take profits too fast, and stay with losers from fear/indecision.

You don't always have to be in the market. Don't trade if you don't feel like it or if it doesn't feel right. You need confidence AND desire to trade. Exceptional traders have both most of the time; for others, they come together only occasionally.

If you can't take a small loss, sooner or later you will take the mother of all losses. Hussein's invasion of Kuwait parallels a trader holding a losing position — each day's procrastination resulted in more destruction. Cut losses early.

Lipschutz: Always think about how much you can LOSE first, not how much you can make. Before any trade, consider: what is the maximum I could lose on this? If you can't define the risk, don't take the trade.

Lipschutz: Trade around a core position. Have a main position you believe in fundamentally, then actively trade portions around it. This allows capturing short-term moves while maintaining long-term exposure.

McKay: When you're in sync with the market, increase size. When out of sync, decrease size dramatically. The market tells you when you're in rhythm — listen to it. Let your equity curve guide your position sizing.

Eckhardt: Don't optimize for the past — optimize for robustness. A system that performs well across many parameter sets is more robust than one that shines at one specific setting. Robustness over perfection.
Eckhardt: Position sizing is more important than entry signals. The same entry signals with different position sizing methods produce wildly different results. Focus on HOW MUCH, not just WHEN.
Trout: Be mechanical about entries and exits but use discretion about position sizing and which markets to trade. The human element should be in the risk management, not the signal generation.

Trout: Track volatility-adjusted performance. If risk per trade is constant, volatile markets produce more extreme results. Normalize your risk by volatility — equal risk per trade in volatility-adjusted terms.

Weiss: The best indicators are the simple ones — moving averages, breakouts. Complexity doesn't equal edge. Simple trend-following systems work because they capture the persistent tendency of markets to trend.

Druckenmiller: It's not whether you're right or wrong that's important, but how much money you make when you're right and how much you lose when you wrong. The asymmetry of outcomes matters more than win rate.

Druckenmiller: The way to build long-term returns is through preservation of capital and home runs. When you have tremendous conviction on a trade, you have to go for the jugular. Cut losses, let winners run to the maximum.

Driehaus: Buy stocks that are already going up rather than looking for stocks that might go up. The best-performing stocks tend to continue performing well. Don't buy on weakness — buy on strength. Momentum works.

Blake: Consistency comes from having an edge and executing it repeatedly. Don't look for home runs. Small consistent gains compound into enormous returns. The math of compounding small edges is powerful.

Sperandeo: Markets grow old too. A long-running trend will eventually end. Watch for the character of the market to change — increased volatility, failed breakouts, loss of momentum. These signal trend exhaustion.

Basso: Risk management IS the system. The entry signal is secondary. Focus on: how much to risk per trade, when to increase/decrease size, and how to handle correlated positions. This is the real edge.
Raschke: Read the music of the markets. Price action patterns repeat because human behavior repeats. Learn to read the pattern in real-time rather than after the fact. Experience teaches the rhythm.
Ritchie (Mark): When the market is in gear — when your analysis, the price action, and the momentum all align — that is when to press. The rest of the time, preserve capital. Wait for the alignment.

Ritchie (Joe): The best traders think in probabilities. They don't need to be right on any individual trade. They focus on the expected value across many trades. Each trade is just one roll of the dice.

Hull: Getting the edge in options means understanding that implied volatility is often mispriced. Selling overpriced volatility and buying underpriced volatility provides a statistical edge over time.
Yass: The mathematics of strategy matter more than the story. Calculate expected value mathematically. If the math doesn't work, no amount of narrative will save the trade. Quantify everything.

Seykota: Everybody gets what they want out of the market. Some people seem to want to lose, so they win by losing. Examine what you truly want — your results will reflect your deepest desires, not your stated ones.

Magazine covers are contrary indicators. 'Are Interest Rates Going to 20%?' = bond market bottom. 'How High Can Oil Prices Go?' = oil top. Read the covers, forget the articles inside. Seykota's media indicator.

Don't trade on someone else's opinion. When you get in on another's analysis, you have no basis for deciding when to get out. You surrender the decision process, eliminating risk control. The market punishes all trading transgressions.

Never hold a position that gaps sharply against you right after entry. A gap against you means something has changed. Get out. You can always reassess and reenter. The gap is a signal the trade premise may be invalid.

Faulkner: The mind of an achiever focuses on process, not outcome. Traders who focus on doing the right thing rather than making money paradoxically make more money. Process orientation beats outcome orientation.

Krausz: The subconscious mind processes market information faster than conscious analysis. Gut feelings in experienced traders are the subconscious recognizing patterns. Trust your trained intuition but verify with rules.

Walton: Don't get so caught up in details that you miss the big picture. Exhaustive stock research means nothing if you miss that the sector is already up 70%. Maintain whole-picture perspective: sector, market, macro.

Walton: Anyone can do this job — you don't have to be born to it. But you need 100% energy and enthusiasm. When emotional upheaval (divorce, personal crisis) interferes, step away. Don't trade when you can't give it full focus.

Walton: Deliberate isolation prevents bad influences. After going wrong by listening to tips and opinions, realized importance of not being influenced by others. Trade alone. No assistants, no analysts, no tips.

The worst thing for a beginning trader is to have your first trade work. It creates overconfidence and reckless behavior. Walton lost all of $10K borrowed from father within 3 weeks of initial winning trade.

Lauer: Value investing means buying when there's a significant gap between price and intrinsic value with a margin of safety. Never pay up. The discipline to wait for the right price is the hardest part.

Watson: Fundamental research on individual stocks combined with strict risk management produces consistent returns. Deep dive into company financials, competitive position, and management quality before entry.
Galante: Go against the current. When everyone is buying, be cautious. When everyone is selling, look for opportunities. Contrarian value approach with strict risk management on each position.

Cook: In S&P trading, risk management is everything. Define your maximum daily loss. When hit, stop trading for the day. Never let one bad day turn into a catastrophic week. Daily loss limits are non-negotiable.

Cook: Track your trades meticulously. Log every entry, exit, and reason. Review weekly. Patterns in your trading errors become visible only through systematic record-keeping. Self-knowledge comes from data.

Fletcher: Win-win investing structures trades to eliminate or minimize downside. Use options, hedging, and structured positions that have asymmetric payoff profiles. Protect capital first, profits second.

Okumus: Focus on stocks with strong earnings growth and relative strength. Buy leading stocks in leading sectors. Cut losses quickly at 7-8%. Let winners run. Use CAN SLIM-type criteria for stock selection.

Minervini: Risk no more than 1% of portfolio on any single stock. Cut losses at 7-8% maximum. The key is keeping losses small. You can be wrong 70% of the time and still be profitable if your winners are much larger than losers.

Minervini: Buy stocks in Stage 2 uptrends — price above 200-day MA, 200-day MA rising, 150-day MA above 200-day MA, 50-day MA above both. Volume should increase on up days. This filters for institutional accumulation.
Minervini: Trade around the clock means continuous research and preparation. The actual trading is 10% of the work. 90% is research, screening, and planning. Preparation is the edge.

Lescarbeau: The ultimate trading system is one you can execute consistently. A mediocre system with perfect execution beats a perfect system with poor execution. Focus on execution discipline above all.

Masters: Swimming through the markets means being flexible across sectors and strategies. Don't be married to one approach. Adapt to what the market is giving you. The market dictates the strategy, not you.
Bender: Question the obvious. When everyone agrees on a stock or sector thesis, ask what they might be missing. The consensus view is already priced in. Edge comes from seeing what others overlook.
Guazzoni: Eliminating the downside means structuring positions where you can't lose much but can gain a lot. Asymmetric risk/reward. If you protect the downside, the upside takes care of itself.

Shaw: Quantitative edge comes from systematic analysis of hundreds of variables simultaneously. No human can process what a computer can. Use quantitative methods to find statistical edges, then execute systematically.

Cohen: In a trading room, the best risk management is honest communication. Traders must share losses openly, not hide them. Hidden losses compound into disasters. Transparency prevents catastrophic surprises.

Kiev: The mind of a winner accepts uncertainty. Great traders don't need to be right — they need to make good decisions under uncertainty. Fear of being wrong is the biggest obstacle to trading success.

Kiev: Performance anxiety creates self-sabotage. Traders who put too much pressure on themselves to perform will choke. Relax, trust your process, and let the results come naturally. Pressure kills performance.

Both long and short sides must be equally OK psychologically. Can't be perennial bull or bear. If first big profit was on long side, traders tend to be permanently biased bullish — very bad. Dennis' imprinting observation.

In our research, if a system doesn't work for both bonds AND beans, we don't care about it. A system should work across different markets. If it only works in one market, it's probably curve-fit. Dennis' robustness test.

Markets trend only about 15% of the time; the rest they move sideways. This means most of your trades during sideways periods will be small losses or breakevens. Accept this — the 15% trending time generates the profits.

PTJ learned from Las Vegas: keep only X dollars in pocket, never have credit. Worst thing is sending good money after bad. If you physically remove yourself from the premises (go flat), you see things in a different perspective.

Druckenmiller's approach: develop a strong thesis, start with a small probe, add aggressively when confirmed. If wrong on the probe, the loss is tiny. If right, the large position generates outsized returns. Asymmetric position building.

When you're getting beat to death, get your head out of the mixer. Dennis: after compounding a loss with three reversals in one day (losing 1/3 of capital), learned to put time between a destabilizing loss and the next decision.

Generals always fight the last war. Portfolio managers always invest in the last bull market. Gold was the 1970s inflation hedge — that doesn't mean it will be the next one. Each era has different winners. Rogers' rule.

The leading cause of financial disablement is believing you can rely on experts. Your average broker couldn't be a trader in a million years. More money is lost listening to brokers than any other way. Do your own homework. Marcus.

If you have eight highly correlated positions, you are really trading one position that is eight times as large. Correlation errors are the root of the most serious trading problems. Daily computer analysis of position correlations is essential.

Kovner's best trading occurred when collaborating with Marcus — they did phenomenal trading together. Two minds checking each other's analysis, challenging assumptions, and confirming views produces better results than solo analysis.

Trading skills can only be taught to a limited extent. Kovner trained ~30 people, only 4-5 became good traders. The difference was not intelligence — it was being strong, independent, and contrary in the extreme. Willingness to take positions others won't.

A greedy trader always blows out. Kovner knew brilliant traders who knew markets better than he did, but he kept money and they didn't. The difference: position size. For every 1 contract Kovner traded, they traded 10.

Don't try to bully the market. Traders who think too highly of their ability and try to push markets ultimately go under. Trading large can work short-term but leads to arrogance and loss of touch with market structure.

The more a price pattern is observed by speculators, the more prone to false signals. The more a market is driven by nonspeculative activity, the greater the significance of technical breakouts. Kovner on signal quality.

When managing larger size, diversification across decision-makers becomes essential. Multiple strategies and multiple decision-makers allow handling several hundred million without major problems. Dennis expanded capacity by training turtles.

Slippage estimation must be hard-nosed and realistic when building cost of trading into a system. Reduce costs by having own brokers. System performance without realistic slippage is fantasy. Dennis on execution costs.

Oct 1987: market gapped past stop levels by 240 points vs normal 40-50 point slippage. When market is that out of line, get out ASAP. If you have any doubt about getting out fast in such a situation, you are in big trouble. Dennis.

Steinhardt's average exposure was 40% net long over 21 years vs typical mutual fund at 80%+. Flexibility to shift market exposure is an exceptionally meaningful tool. Range: 15-20% net short to over 100% net long.

As soon as a formula is right for any length of time, its own success carries the weight of its inevitable failure. No set pattern of successful activity lasts. Anyone who thinks he can formulate success is deluding himself. Steinhardt.

When market has enormous move, most of the time it is right to view it as emotional and extreme. Maintain distance from the emotionalism. Steinhardt bought heavily on Oct 19, 1987 crash day — contrarian trade on extreme move.

I look at the basic industry: supply/demand cycles. Tanker rates, shipping, commodities follow classic cycles. Prices get high → everyone builds ships → prices go down → ships scrapped → prices go back up. Find where in the cycle you are.

Trade the Australian dollar rather than Deutsche mark. Trade the small OTC stock rather than big Dow stock. Better to trade where professional sharks aren't eating each other. Less sophisticated markets offer bigger edges.

When government measures counteract a trend, the government action provides temporary relief but the trend resumes. Prop up a currency = sell the rally. Support a market = fade the intervention. Intervention is noise, trend is signal.

Walton: If you can't give trading 100% energy and enthusiasm, don't trade. Walk away. He returned all investor money and stopped trading completely during a divorce. The discipline to NOT trade is as important as the discipline to trade.

Cook: After a devastating loss, shrink positions to 1/5 to 1/10 normal. Focus on getting back to profitability with small wins. Don't try to make it all back at once. The recovery process takes patience and humility.

Minervini: The Volatility Contraction Pattern (VCP) — price consolidates with decreasing volatility (tightening range) after a run-up. The contraction represents institutional accumulation. Breakout from VCP with volume surge is the buy signal.

Minervini: Position sizing based on volatility. More volatile stocks get smaller positions. If a stock moves 5% daily, position should be half the size of one that moves 2.5% daily. Normalize risk by volatility across all positions.

Gut feel is very important. No great professional trader lacks it. Being a successful trader also takes courage: courage to try, courage to fail, courage to succeed, and courage to keep going when the going gets tough. Marcus on intuition.

When your feelings as a trader conflict with your system, do nothing until you resolve that conflict. Don't override your system impulsively, but don't ignore valid intuition either. Stand aside until clarity returns. Dennis.

Dennis' friend lost mansion and everything on one gold trade. Took system sell signal, ignored it, went long instead, market went down, didn't get out, lost it all. If you have a system, FOLLOW IT. One deviation can be catastrophic.

Hite on position sizing: We don't trade markets, we trade money. Cocoa and gold are both 1% bets — they are the same. The instrument doesn't matter, only the risk/reward ratio of the bet. Trade money, not stories.

Impulsive (not intuitive) trades have the highest failure rate. Once a strategy is selected, stick to your game plan. Avoid unplanned trades from friend recommendations or sudden urges. Kovner's 'going bust trade' was impulsive.
Seidler: Focus on the process, not the money. When you focus on executing well, the money follows. When you focus on money, you make emotional decisions. Process focus produces consistent execution.

Druckenmiller: When you have high conviction, size matters. Having a great thesis and putting on a tiny position is almost as bad as being wrong. If you've done the work and the setup is right, bet meaningfully.
Druckenmiller: Focus on what the Fed is doing. Monetary policy drives markets more than any other single factor. When the Fed is easing, be long. When tightening, be cautious. Don't fight the Fed.

Rogers: I haven't met a rich technician. Charts show what HAS happened, not what WILL happen. Use charts for knowledge about what is going on, but make trading decisions on fundamental supply/demand analysis.

Never have more than 5% in one idea. Related positions count as one idea. Two grain markets long = one idea. The purpose is to survive 20+ consecutive wrong trades. Even great traders have extended losing streaks.

Trading is emotion. It is mass psychology, greed, and fear. It is all the same in every situation and every market. If you can trade one market, you can trade them all — the principles are universal. Marcus on market universality.

Kovner: Long-term scenarios can be right or wrong, but even if right, they may not matter over the term of any individual trade. Don't let a macro view override your risk management on individual positions.

Kovner: Rigid long-term views are most likely to cause you to miss a major profit opportunity. If you believe dollar will weaken but get a buy signal, missing the profit to avoid a small loss means terrible risk/reward.

Hite's friend lost everything on one gold trade — took sell signal, didn't follow it, went long, refused to exit. Lost the mansion. If you follow the system and take the sell signal, you make a fortune instead. System discipline is survival.

Work, work, and more work. Schwartz's bottom line. Trading requires intense personal involvement. You have to do your own homework. The more effort you put into preparation, the better your execution will be.

Shaw: Quantitative risk models must account for tail risk — the rare events that don't appear in normal distributions. 10-sigma events happen more often than models predict. Always prepare for the worst case.
Cohen: Edge in trading comes from information advantage — not inside information, but processing public information faster and more accurately than others. Speed of analysis is a legitimate edge.

Both long and short sides must be psychologically equal. Can't feel better about buying than selling. If first major win imprinted you as permanent bull or bear, your trading will go askew. Dennis on psychological balance.

When central banks take other side of currency trend, it prevents trends from developing. This makes currency trend-following much harder in modern markets. Marcus' observation that trend era may be over.

Every trader has strengths and weaknesses. Some are good holders of winners but hold losers too long. Others cut winners short but take losses quickly. As long as you stick to YOUR style, you get the good and bad of your approach.

The best trades are when you have all three things: fundamentals, technicals, and market tone. When all three align, enter 5-6x your normal position. The other trades should just break even and keep you amused. Marcus.

Managed money offers potential return with no risk — it's like a call option. Dennis' rationale for managing outside money. The maximum loss is zero (your own capital), but the upside is unlimited through performance fees.

Don't just go with optimal parameter set from backtesting. If the suboptimal set is only 10% worse historically but fits the future better, deliberately trade the suboptimal version. Dennis on parameter selection.

Steptoe (Weinstein): Never have a specific dollar target for a trade. Let market conditions determine exit. Having a target like '$350K for a castle' distorts position sizing and risk assessment. Trade the market, not your desires.

Limit-up rules trap traders in losing positions. Weinstein lost $600K+ over 5 consecutive limit-down days in soybeans unable to exit. Price limits protect the exchange, not the trader. Be aware of limit risk in futures.

There are people in this business who enjoy watching others lose money. Weinstein's colleagues were relieved and mocked his soybean loss. Trading environment can be toxic. Choose your social environment carefully.

Baldwin: Fearless pit traders make money by being first to react. Speed of execution and decisiveness matter. When you see the signal, act. Hesitation costs money. But be fearless about the PROCESS, not the money.

Saliba: One-lot triumphs — you can build a fortune starting with the smallest possible position. The key is consistency and compounding. Start small, prove your method, then scale. Size follows success, not precedes it.

Dr. Van Tharp: Trading success is determined more by psychology than methodology. Two traders with the same system will get different results based on their psychological makeup. Work on yourself as much as your system.

Tharp: The quality of your trading is proportional to the quality of your self-knowledge. Know your biases, your triggers, your emotional patterns. Self-awareness is the foundation of consistent trading performance.
Lipschutz: Missing an opportunity is as bad as being on the wrong side of a trade. When you feel strongly about a setup, dont let the bid/ask spread prevent execution.

Lipschutz: What matters is what the market is focusing on RIGHT NOW. One day its interest rate differentials, next day capital appreciation. Same news bullish or bearish depending on current narrative.
Lipschutz: FX trading is all about relationships. Best-connected traders get best fills and best information. Be personable, call dealers by name.
Eckhardt: Never risk more than 2% on a single trade. Trading size at high end of linear performance range. Beyond optimum, performance plummets — even modestly too large is negative.
Eckhardt: Human nature teaches POOR trading. Small profits vanish so market teaches cash in early. Consolidations teach buy dips. Returns to old prices teach hold losers. Opposite of correct.
Eckhardt: Amateurs go broke taking large losses. Professionals go broke taking small profits. Success rate is LEAST important stat, possibly inversely related to performance.
Eckhardt: The call of the countertrend — people view accustomed prices as normal, prices away as aberrant. This leads to trading counter to emerging trends. Path to disaster.
Eckhardt: Losses make you strong, profits make you weak. Large profits more insidious than large losses for emotional destabilization. After wins you think you are special and make shoddy decisions.
Eckhardt: Do not think about scenarios where market goes your way. Focus on what you want LEAST to happen and your response. Think contingencies, not fantasies.
Eckhardt: Best research breakthroughs came during losing periods. Channel worry into research. Use drawdowns as catalysts for improvement.
Eckhardt: 95%+ of charting contest contestants scored worse than random. Majority trade worse than random. Market teaches poor techniques. What works most of time is opposite of long run.
Eckhardt: If it feels good, dont do it. When all criteria in balance, do the thing you LEAST want to do. Natural instincts mislead. Reprogram to do correct over comfortable.
Eckhardt: Survivors avoid snowball scenarios where bad trades cause emotional destabilization. If losses dont hurt, financial survival is tenuous. Pain is survival mechanism.
Seidler: Have a PLAN. Plan forces discipline and gives benchmark. You can follow rules and still lose — thats not poor performance. Conformance to plan more significant than short-term equity.
Seidler: Cannot win at leverage that makes you fearful. Reduce position until you can think clearly and objectively. Fear distorts judgment.
Trading is highly addictive — intermittent reinforcement is most addictive schedule. Addictiveness is why many who make fortunes leave the game broke.
Eckhardt: Believe system results only after trying to disprove them. Great simulated results often from errors. More closely system fits past data, worse future performance.
Eckhardt: Rather bet on someone starting with few thousand than millions. People who dont feel pain of losses dont survive. Inherited wealth traders lost it all.
Carr (Turtle): Dont worry about what markets will do — worry about what YOU will do in response. Focus on reactions, not predictions. Your response is only controllable.
Eckhardt: Anyone with average intelligence can learn to trade. But easier to learn what to do than to do it. Good systems violate normal human tendencies.
Walton: Returned $150M to investors when personal life interfered. Never manage money when you cannot give 100% energy. Discipline to NOT trade is as important as trading.
Walton: 115% avg annual return over 8 years working completely alone. Deliberate isolation prevents bad influences. Independent thinking is essential.
Cook: Define maximum daily loss. When hit, stop trading. Never let one bad day become catastrophic week. Daily loss limits non-negotiable. Log every trade.
Minervini: Buy Stage 2 uptrends — price above 200-day MA, 200-day rising, 150 above 200, 50 above both. Volume increases on up days. Filters institutional accumulation.
Minervini: Risk 1% per stock max. Cut losses at 7-8%. Can be wrong 70% of time and still profitable if winners much larger. Size by volatility.
Minervini: VCP — price consolidates with decreasing volatility. Tightening range = institutional accumulation. Breakout with volume surge = buy signal.
Cohen: Edge from processing public info faster. Speed of analysis is legitimate edge. Honest communication about losses in trading room prevents disasters.
Kiev: Great traders dont need to be right — need good decisions under uncertainty. Fear of being wrong is biggest obstacle. Trust process.
Bender: Question the obvious. Consensus view already priced in. Edge from seeing what others overlook.
Fletcher: Structure trades for asymmetric payoff. Use options/hedging to eliminate downside. Protect capital first, profits second.
Lipschutz: Zero tolerance for operational errors. Back-office error budget = ZERO. Enough fail-safes = no errors. Operational discipline as important as trading discipline.
Lipschutz: Make long-term business decisions, not short-term profit decisions. Short-term profit maximization destroys long-term relationships and marketplace.
Carr: People caught up in excitement of wager amount destabilized by losses. Need detached view of money value to trade successfully.
Raschke: Price patterns repeat because human behavior repeats. Learn to read patterns in real-time. Market has rhythm — experience teaches you to hear the music.
Druckenmiller: High conviction = meaningful size. Great thesis with tiny position almost as bad as being wrong. Asymmetric position building with probes.
Druckenmiller: Focus on Fed actions. Monetary policy drives markets more than any other factor. Fed easing = be long. Tightening = be cautious.
Druckenmiller: Not whether right or wrong but how much you make when right vs lose when wrong. Asymmetry matters more than win rate.
Lauer: Value investing with margin of safety. Buy when gap between price and intrinsic value is significant. Never pay up. Discipline to wait is hardest part.
Shaw: Quantitative edge from analyzing hundreds of variables simultaneously. Account for tail risk — rare events happen more than models predict.
Kiev: Performance anxiety creates self-sabotage. Too much pressure to perform causes choking. Relax, trust process, let results come naturally.
